coredata.dc:title: Construction of graphene quantum dot-based dissolving microneedle patches for the treatment of bacterial keratitis
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Yirong
Zhuo, Lin
Yuan, Hang
Zhao, Hao
Zhang, Lishu
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial keratitis (BK) is an ophthalmic infection caused by bacteria and poses a risk of blindness. Numerous drugs have been used to treat BK, the majority suffered from limited effect owing to their backward antimicrobial and delivery efficacy. Herein, we evaluated the antibacterial effect of a cationic carbon-based nanomaterial, i.e., imidazole-modified graphene quantum dots (IMZ-GQDs), which exhibits disinfection rates of >90% against three typical Gram-positive strains within 3 h owing to the loss of membrane integrity and decline in membrane potential. For ocular application, we further developed IMZ-GQDs-loaded dissolving microneedle patches (IMZ-GQDs MNs) via a typical two-step micromolding method. IMZ-GQDs MNs showed sufficient dissolution and penetration for intrastromal delivery in vitro and successfully overcome the rabbit corneal epithelial layer in vivo. The excellent biocompatibility of IMZ-GQDs MNs was demonstrated both in cell and animal models, and they exhibited low cytotoxicity, low invasiveness and low ocular irritation. The topical application of IMZ-GQDs MNs has the benefits of both high antibacterial activity and effective drug delivery, thereby leading to the resolution of Staphylococcus aureus-induced BK in rabbits in 7 days. Therefore, IMZ-GQDs MNs is a promising approach for BK treatment, which is safe and efficient.

head.ce:title: Construction of graphene quantum dot-based dissolving microneedle patches for the treatment of bacterial keratitis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, Eye Hospital, Wenzhou Medical University, 270 Xueyuan Xi Road, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325027, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel dissolving microneedle patches loaded with cationic antibacterial agent, i.e., imidazole-modified graphene quantum dots in PVA/PVP matrix (IMZ-GQDs MNs) with improved antibacterial activity and bioavailability was synthesized by a typical two-step micromolding method.

Bacterial keratitis (BK) is an ophthalmic infection caused by bacteria and poses a risk of blindness. Numerous drugs have been used to treat BK, the majority suffered from limited effect owing to their backward antimicrobial and delivery efficacy. Herein, we evaluated the antibacterial effect of a cationic carbon-based nanomaterial, i.e., imidazole-modified graphene quantum dots (IMZ-GQDs), which exhibits disinfection rates of >90% against three typical Gram-positive strains within 3 h owing to the loss of membrane integrity and decline in membrane potential. For ocular application, we further developed IMZ-GQDs-loaded dissolving microneedle patches (IMZ-GQDs MNs) via a typical two-step micromolding method. IMZ-GQDs MNs showed sufficient dissolution and penetration for intrastromal delivery in vitro and successfully overcome the rabbit corneal epithelial layer in vivo. The excellent biocompatibility of IMZ-GQDs MNs was demonstrated both in cell and animal models, and they exhibited low cytotoxicity, low invasiveness and low ocular irritation. The topical application of IMZ-GQDs MNs has the benefits of both high antibacterial activity and effective drug delivery, thereby leading to the resolution of Staphylococcus aureus-induced BK in rabbits in 7 days. Therefore, IMZ-GQDs MNs is a promising approach for BK treatment, which is safe and efficient.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial keratitis (BK) is a common ophthalmic disease and a major cause of corneal opacity (Ting et al., 2021). Globally, in 2015, corneal opacity was ranked as the fifth most common cause of blindness and moderate-to-severe vision impairment (Flaxman et al., 2017). Infectious keratitis has been shown to be the leading cause of corneal blindness in China at present, with BK accounting for most of the cases (Ting et al., 2021; Xiusheng, 2014). The bacteria first engage with the corneal epithelial cells via adhesions to initiate epithelial infections and defects, with acute pain, redness, and photophobia as clinical presentations (Jett and Gilmore, 2002; Lakhundi et al., 2017; O'Brien, 2003; Stapleton et al., 2007). Subsequently, the pathogen invades the corneal stroma and releases exotoxins to degrade the cellular components, thus resulting in corneal ulceration or perforation and even endophthalmitis, increasing the risk of blindness or even systemic infections (Zhang et al., 2017). Undoubtedly, the prevention and treatment of BK are important issues for clinicians and researchers.

In BK, Gram-positive bacteria account for a relatively higher proportion of detected pathogenic bacteria, with Staphylococcus epidermidis (S. epidermidis) being the most common, followed by Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) (Chandra et al., 2021; Soleimani et al., 2021; Walkden et al., 2018). The first-line treatment for BK is normally the high-dose, high-frequency administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, cephalosporins, etc.) in the conventional form of eye drop/ointment or invasive forms, such as corneal stromal and subconjunctival injections, which may cause cytotoxicity to normal tissue at a high dosage (Gokhale, 2008; Kamoshita et al., 2015; Li et al., 2011; Wróblewska et al., 2021). Additionally, the prolonged overuse of antibiotics has significantly increased the number of drug-resistant strains of pathogenic bacteria, including multidrug-resistant strains, followed by failure of medication (Goldstein et al., 1999). The abovementioned factors limit the therapeutic efficacy of current drug treatment for BK. When the medication fails to prevent the progression of infection, surgical intervention is required for vision preservation. Moreover, eye removal may be needed to avoid systemic infection, which can be both physically and mentally taxing for the patient (Fuchsluger et al., 2007; Iyer et al., 2006; Said et al., 2021). Therefore, there is an urgent need for a safer and more effective approach to treat BK.

Antibacterial nanomaterials have emerged recently and have quickly become a hotspot in various fields of biomedicine. These materials have gained attention owing to the boom of nanoscience and are expected to break the deadlock in the antibacterial therapy of BK (Makabenta et al., 2021). The intrinsic resistance of bacteria is known to evolve from genetic modifications during their long-term struggle with antibiotics and is manifested as reduced susceptibility, increased efflux, and corresponding mutations in metabolic genes (Blair et al., 2015; Tenover, 2006). Owing to their incomparable size and structural advantages, nanomaterials evade existing resistance mechanisms via multiple antibacterial channels, which are as follows: membrane disruption (Nederberg et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2013), binding to intracellular components (Zhao et al., 2010), generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Dong et al., 2020; Miller et al., 2015), and therapeutics delivery (Abdelghany et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2019). Carbon-based nanomaterials are a board subclass of antibacterial nanomaterials, which are more biocompatible and environmentally friendly than metal nanomaterials (Alavi et al., 2021; Alayande et al., 2019; Bellingeri et al., 2018; Dogan-Guner et al., 2019; Li et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2016). Graphene is a single-layer carbon-based nanomaterial, with the ordered two-dimensional (2D) planar structure consisting of carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice with sp2 hybridization. This arrangement contributes to the extremely high intrinsic mobility of charge carriers, zero band gap, large surface area, and high chemical stability (Benítez-Martínez and Valcárcel, 2015; Geim, 2009). Graphene and its derivatives have been widely accepted for antibacterial usage. However, pure graphene tends to be poorly soluble and nonluminescent (Yan et al., 2010). Therefore, the modification of graphene has been a hot topic among the scientific community in recent years.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type of zero-dimensional (0D) graphene-based materials with extremely small nanoparticles (2–20 nm, smaller than the Bohr radius), exhibit strong fluorescence properties owing to quantum confinement effects (Henna and Pramod, 2020; Xu et al., 2013). Soon after the emergence of GQDs, researchers found that they are especially applicable in biological fields because of their suitable optoelectronic properties, excellent biocompatibility, high water solubility, easy modification, and low toxicity (Arvand and Hemmati, 2017; Chung et al., 2021). The antimicrobial applications of GQDs, mainly via irritations of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) or photodynamic therapy (PDT), have also been reported; however, the extra stresses may not be appropriate for sensitive tissues, such as the ocular surface (Kuo et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2014). Therefore, the development of safer antimicrobial GQDs is the need of the hour. Imidazole, one of the most common five-membered heterocyclic compounds in nature, participates in multiple enzymatic reactions in the human body and plays a pivotal role in the pharmaceutical field (Bistrović et al., 2018; Fan et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). The hydrophilic cationic segments originating from imidazole alkylation, initially induce electrostatic interaction with the negatively charged bacterial surface, followed by structural perturbation, resulting in bacterial cell death (El Badawy et al., 2011; Gao et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2016; Riduan and Zhang, 2013). Huang et al. (Jian et al., 2017; Jian et al., 2020) have prepared a series of cationic carbon-based nanomaterials for the prevention and treatment of BK. However, carbon-based antibacterial nanomaterials modified with imidazole have been rarely reported. Additionally, the bioavailability of nanoparticles remains a considerable challenge. In the conventional form of eye drop/ointment, drugs fail to penetrate the anatomic and physiologic barrier to achieve effective therapeutic concentrations (Huang et al., 2018). Micro drug delivery systems implanted intraocularly could achieve controlled release of drugs, however, their use is associated with the risk of surgical intervention (Yasukawa et al., 2006). Therefore, safe, efficient, and controlled ocular drug delivery modes have been a common concern for both researchers and clinicians.

Microneedles (MNs) are microdevices consisting of micron-sized needles arranged on a small patch, thus providing a novel drug delivery mode that is safe and effective (Kim et al., 2012). MNs were originally developed for transdermal drug delivery via the stratum corneum with their micron-sized tips and have been well studied in epidermal anesthesia, blood glucose monitoring, vaccination, subcutaneous tumor treatment, and infection treatment (Donadei et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2008; Ohn et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2019). Of these, dissolving MNs could be prepared with various biodegradable polymers, including polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), which dissolve quickly without sharp residuals when they come in contact with body fluids (Gupta and Yadav, 2019; Lev-Tov, 2017). The merits of MNs are also applicable in the in the field of ophthalmology, thus making them a viable option to improve the ocular bioavailability of drugs.

In the present study, we developed dissolving microneedle patches (IMZ-GQDs MNs) by encapsulating IMZ-GQDs in PVA/PVP matrix via a typical two-step micromolding method, which aimed to improve the therapeutic effect of BK (Scheme 1 ). Specifically, before the fabrication of MNs, the antibacterial performance of IMZ-GQDs towards typical Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus, S. epidermidis, and methicillin-resistant S. aureus, i.e., MRSA) and typical Gram-positive bacteria Escherichia coli (E. coli) was tested, followed by verifying the relationship between the antibacterial effect and cationic feature of IMZ-GQDs. After evaluation of the antibacterial ingredient, the optimized morphology, dissolution, and penetration of IMZ-GQDs MNs ensure their invasiveness to the corneal stroma. The antibacterial mechanism of IMZ-GQDs was explored via zeta potential measurement and membrane potential staining. In addition, IMZ-GQDs MNs exhibit high biocompatibility with no apparent cell or ocular irritation. In a rabbit BK model (S. aureus-induced), IMZ-GQDs MNs were demonstrated to be therapeutically effective compared with commercial antibiotics and other delivery modes. As a result, IMZ-GQDs MNs have demonstrated a promising scheme for the safe, convenient and efficient treatment of BK. We believe this type of approach will revolutionize the way that numerous superficial diseases have been treated for long periods of time.

Bacterial growth and assays were performed using procedures detailed in supplementary data. The colony-forming unit (CFU) counting method was applied to determine the cytotoxicity of IMZ-GQDs (XF-203, XFNANO, Nanjing) toward bacteria. S. aureus was selected to estimate the bactericidal activities at different IMZ-GQDs concentrations. First, IMZ-GQDs suspensions were separately added at final concentrations of 0, 50, 100, and 200 µg/mL (in phosphate buffered saline, PBS) to bacterial culture tubes that contained equal amounts of S. aureus solution (106 CFU/ mL, 5 mL). Next, mixtures of different material concentrations were cultured for 3 h (37 °C, 200 rpm). At 0 and 3 h, 100-µL suspension from each group was inoculated in a sterile Eppendorf tube and then diluted in a gradient of 1/10 with PBS until the appropriate bacterial concentration was reached (30–300 CFU/plate). Later, 100-µL microbial dilutions were spread on Luria–Bertani agar plates in triplicate and cultured for 24 h until visible colonies were for counting were obtained. The antibacterial activities of IMZ-GQDs at different concentrations were evaluated by comparing the CFUs (three replicates) before and after coculture in each group.

S. aureus, S. epidermidis, MRSA, and E. coli were used as experimental strains to evaluate the in vitro antibacterial effects of IMZ-GQDs on different bacteria. For each strain, the control and IMZ-GQDs-treated groups (100 µg/mL in PBS) were established, with 106 CFU/mL of bacterial solution in the total 5 mL mixture. Each group was incubated for 3 h (37 °C, 200 rpm). After 0, 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 h of incubation, 100 µL of the mixture was taken from the control and experimental groups, diluted in three gradients (1/10), and spread on the plates (three replicates) for further counting and analysis. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) or transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images of the bacteria and live/dead assay were performed as steps listed in the supplementary information.

Zeta potential and antimicrobial properties of IMZ-GQDs and aminated GQDs (XF-093, XFNANO, Nanjing) are tested according to the steps in the supplementary information. The bacterial membrane potentials were measured using BacLight™ Bacterial Membrane Potential Kit (L34950, Thermal Fisher, USA). Three tubes of S. aureus suspension (106 CFU/mL, 1 mL) were prepared for the IMZ-GQDs-treated, depolarized control, and unstained control groups. Of these, the IMZ-GQDs-treated suspension was incubated (37℃, 200 rpm) with 100 µg/mL of IMZ-GQDs in PBS for 3 h, and the other two controls were cultivated naturally. After coculture, 10 µL of 500-µM 3-chlorophenylhydrazone (CCCP) was added to one of the untreated bacterial suspensions as the depolarized control (positive control). Immediately after the addition of CCCP, three tubes (1 mL) were incubated with DiOC2(3) (10 µL, 3 mM) at room temperature for 30 min. The mixtures (1.0 mL) were then centrifuged (8000 rpm, 5 min) and washed three times with PBS to remove the extra dye solution. Subsequently, 10-µL resuspension was dropped on glass slides and covered with an 18 × 18-mm slip for a clear view. For the quantification and analysis of bacterial membrane potentials, the stained bacteria were observed and photographed using a Dmi8inverted fluorescent microscope. The red/green ratios were calculated using the mean intensities of green and red fluorescence with ImageJ (National Institutes of Health, USA).

The polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) mold (5 × 5 arrays, tip parameter H500 B250 P500) was designed and manufactured based on relevant reports (Bui et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2019). The reported effective dosage of IMZ-GQDs is 6 µg for a single eye. To ensure that the theoretical loading of a single MN reaches this concentration, we vacuumed the concentrated IMZ-GQDs solution to 30 mg/mL in a vacuum drying oven (DZF-6096, YIHENG, Shanghai). IMZ-GQDs MNs were fabricated via a typical two-step micromolding method (Ito et al., 2012). Briefly, 25 µL of IMZ-GQDs casting solution was first poured onto the PDMS mold and placed under a vacuum of −0.08 MPa (20℃) for 10 min to facilitate the solution casting process, and the excess solution was removed from the mold surface with a pipette. Next, another 10 min of vacuuming (−0.08 MPa, 20℃) was applied to the IMZ-GQDs casting solution to compress the inner air and concentrate the solution to the tips. Dissolvable MNs were prepared using a combination of PVA (Mw 31,000, Sigma) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, K-30, OKA, Beijing). PVA (15% w/v) and PVP (10% w/v) were dissolved in deionized water and mixed in a ratio of 3:2. Thereafter, approximately a 25-µL PVA/PVP mixture was placed on the PDMS mold, followed by the same vacuum treatment for 10 min. The mold with the solution was dried in a desiccator containing silica gel at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the IMZ-GQDs MNs were obtained by gently peeling them off from the PDMS molds and were stored in a desiccator for later use. Blank MNs were also prepared using the similar fabrication procedure, omitting the addition of IMZ-GQDs.

The optical microscopic images (whole and section views) of IMZ-GQDs MNs and Blank MNs were obtained using a stereo microscope (DM750, Leica, USA). The MNs morphologies and sizes were characterized using SEM with a 70° specimen holder.

The time required for MNs dissolution was studied in 20% ethanol/water (v/v) solution. The MNs tips were dipped into a dissolution medium and withdrawn at different time points (0 s, 30 s, and 2 min). The dissolving condition of the treated MNs was observed and recorded using a DM750 stereo microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity of IMZ-GQDs MNs
ce:section.ce:para: IMZ-GQDs MNs and Blank MNs were dissolved for characterization of in vitro antibacterial activity. Four tubes of S. aureus suspension (106 CFU/mL) were prepared for the Blank control, Blank MNs-treated, IMZ-GQDs-treated, and IMZ-GQDs MNs-treated groups. In vitro antibacterial activities of the abovementioned groups were estimated as described in the previous section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro penetration of IMZ-GQDs MNs
ce:section.ce:para: Parafilm® M (FPF566, Parafilm, USA) was used to validate the in vitro penetration of IMZ-GQDs MNs and Blank MNs. Multiple layers of Parafilm® M were stacked to increase the thickness and were pressed to eliminate gaps between the layers. Subsequently, the prepared MNs were pressed on the stacked Parafilm® M with the tip facing downward for 2 min. After removing the MNs, the Parafilm® M samples were separated in a sequence and marked for observation. The number of pinholes was counted and photographed using a DM750 stereo microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo corneal permeation of IMZ-GQDs MNs
ce:section.ce:para: Care and use of the laboratory animals adhered to the protocol approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the Wenzhou Medical University (Approval number: wydw2021-0236).

The topical application of IMZ-GQDs MNs was checked using a fluorescein sodium staining assay under slit-lamp biomicroscopy (SLM-7E, KANGHUA, Chongqing) to identify any corneal permeation. Briefly, the MNs were topically applied to the ocular surface of rabbits (New Zealand white rabbits, male, 2.5–3.0 kg) by swab pressing for 2 min and then detached. Later, a 0.5% fluorescein sodium (F809553, MACKLIN, Shanghai) solution was added dropwise to the conjunctival sac of treated rabbit eyes for staining, and the excess dye was rinsed with 0.9% NaCl. The integrity and staining of the corneal epithelium were observed and photographed with slit-lamp biomicroscopy at 2 min and 24 h after the application of IMZ-GQDs MNs.
In vitro cytotoxicity and in vivo ocular irritation test of IMZ-GQDs MNs were contained in supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modeling of S. aureus-induced BK in rabbits
ce:section.ce:para: Eighteen adult New Zealand white rabbits (2.5–3.0 kg) were used for the in vivo therapeutic efficacy study. An experimental BK model was induced with an intrastromal injection of 10-µL S. aureus suspension (105 CFU/mL) using 29-gage insulin needles (Kupferman and Leibowitz, 1976). After 6 h of inoculation, the rabbits were randomly categorized into six groups: 1) Control: topical instillation of 0.9% NaCl once a day; 2) Blank MNs: topical application of Blank MNs once a day; 3) IMZ-GQDs eye drop: topical instillation of IMZ-GQDs solution (100 µg/mL) once a day; 4) IMZ-GQDs MNs: topical application of IMZ-GQDs MNs once a day; 5) Ofloxacin0.3% eye drops: topical instillation of commercial ofloxacin eye drops (3 mg/mL) once a day; and 6) Ofloxacin0.01% eye drops: topical instillation of diluted ofloxacin eye drops (100 µg/mL) once a day. Therapeutic efficacy evaluation was performed as using procedures detailed in supplementary data.

SPSS25.0 (IBM, USA) was used to analyze the data, and Origin9 (OriginLab, USA) was used for graphing. All measures were expressed as Mn ± SD with sample means and standard deviations. Data were analyzed by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by t-test. A p-value of<0.05 (P < 0.05) was considered to be statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial effect of IMZ-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of IMZ-GQDs was determined using a simple CFU counting method. As shown in Fig. S1, a significant decrease in the S. aureus survival percentage was observed with the increase in the IMZ-GQDs concentration after coculturing for 3 h. At a concentration of 50 µg/mL, the survival rate of S. aureus dropped to 4.1%; when the solution concentration reached 100 µg/mL, only 0.9% of S. aureus survived. However, when the concentration was doubled again to 200 µg/mL, the survival rate did not drop further and remained at 0.7%, which was not statistically significant compared with the 0.9% at 100 µg/mL concentration. Combined with biosafety considerations, IMZ-GQDs at a concentration of 100 µg/mL were applied in subsequent experiments.

As shown in Fig. 1 and S2–S4 , the bacterial survival rates at different time points were used to understand the effect of IMZ-GQDs on different bacterial strains, including three Gram-positive bacteria strains (S. aureus, S. epidermidis, and MRSA) and one Gram-negative strain (E. coli). In any of the control groups, there was no notable decrease in the survival rate of the four bacterial strains after 3 h of incubation (viability > 90%), thereby suggesting that the influences of the applied culture conditions could be excluded.

For S. aureus (Fig. 1A), which is a representative Gram-positive bacterium and a common causative agent of BK, the viabilities were recorded as 47.6%, 22.5%, 5.7% and 1.8% after 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 h of incubation with IMZ-GQDs, respectively. This observation suggests that the survival rate decreased significantly with the prolonged action of IMZ-GQDs. The corresponding colony images also showed that only single-digit colonies grew on the plates after 3 h of coculture.

As for the other two Gram-positive bacteria, the survival rate demonstrated a similar trend. S. epidermidis, the most common Gram-positive organism in BK, exhibited a 3-h survival rate of 0.4% (Fig. S2 A), with a slight downward trend in the early part of the curve, probably because of its adherent aggregation properties (O'Gara and Humphreys, 2001). MRSA is known as a “superbug” and displays multidrug resistance, with a 3-h survival rate of 0.9% (Fig. S3 A), equivalent to those of the previous two Gram-positive bacteria. The MRSA resistance is mainly due to the regulation of gene expression and enhanced active efflux function, which are extremely dependent on the integrity of the cell wall (JH, 2006; Neu, 1992). IMZ-GQDs may exert their antimicrobial action by disrupting the cell wall, thus depriving MRSA of its drug resistance advantage.

Theresultsobtainedshowed that the survival rates of the three Gram-positive strains decreased below 2% after 3 h of incubation with IMZ-GQDs. However, contrary to the rapid response of the selected Gram-positive strains, E. coli, the representative Gram-negative bacterium, survived much longer in the coculture with IMZ-GQDs (Fig. S4 A). The survival rate of the treated E. coli showed no statistical difference from that of the control group until 2 h of action (81.6%). Even when the reaction time was extended to 3 h, 75.8% of the E. coli cells survived. The corresponding plate colony images also showed that E. coli in both control and treated groups were growing well.

It is clear that IMZ-GQDs do not display the same antibacterial activity against E. coli as that against the three Gram-positive bacteria. The bacteria are classified into Gram-negative and Gram-positive depending on the cell wall structure. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan layer (15–100 nm), and the characteristic teichoic acid is responsible for the negative bacterial charge. On the contrary, the Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer (20–50 nm) and are protected by the outer layer consisting of lipopolysaccharides (LPS). The bacterial membrane is negatively charged primarily because of the presence of phosphates and carboxylates as LPS components (Gupta et al., 2019; Steeghs et al., 2001). These differences in bacterial structure and composition may ultimately lead to the differences in antimicrobial effects. Additionally, Gram-positive bacteria are usually more susceptible to imidazole compounds (Lee et al., 2016; Riduan and Zhang, 2013). The abovementioned factors may result in the varying electrostatic effects and group affinities of IMZ-GQDs against the two types of bacteria, thus affecting their antibacterial effect (Laloux and Jacobs-Wagner, 2014).

The integrity of the bacterial membrane was detected using the SYTO 9/ Propidium Iodide(PI) staining procedure. SYTO 9 and PI are fluorescent nucleic acid dyes that bind to DNA and RNA. SYTO 9 can penetrate the prokaryotic and eukaryotic cell membranes, labeling both live and dead cells with green fluorescence, whereas PI is a cell-impermeable stain that can only fluorescently label cells with broken membranes in red.

As shown in Fig. 1B, S. aureus cells in the control group (Fig. 1B a–c) were evenly distributed and viable, showing very strong green fluorescence. Conversely, S. aureus treated with IMZ-GQDs (100 µg/mL, 3 h) appeared to be agglomerated, with a significant reduction in green fluorescence and a strong red fluorescence (Fig. 1B d–f). This finding implies that a large number of S. aureus cell walls were broken, which allowed PI to enter the cells and stain the bacteria red. Furthermore, similar results were obtained for S. epidermidis (Fig. S2B) and MRSA (Fig. S3B), thereby indicating that IMZ-GQDs exerted antibacterial activities against the three Gram-positive strains via cell membrane disruption.

The staining results of E. coli also matched the abovementioned findings. In Fig. S4B, the E. coli cells remained broadly scattered after IMZ-GQDs treatment, with a relatively strong green fluorescence and weak but widely distributed red fluorescence. Thus, the proportion of broken bacteria was less than that of the Gram-positive bacteria under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM and TEM images of the bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: To further confirm the effect of IMZ-GQDs on the cell wall, the morphological alterations in the bacteria before and after different treatments were studied using SEM and TEM. As shown in the SEM images, the S. aureus cells in the control group (Fig. 2a) presented a typical spherical structure with smooth edges. After treatment with IMZ-GQDs (Fig. 2b), the general outline of S. aureus was preserved, but the surface was severely deformed, with obvious folds and cracks, which is probably due to the thick peptidoglycan layer in the cell wall that prevents the bacterial structure from disintegrating (Gupta et al., 2019). Similar trends were obtained in the other two gram-positive strains (Fig. S2C and S3C) and were consistent with the plate colony counts and fluorescent staining results described above. Additionally, morphological changes in E. coli were not obvious under SEM (Fig. S4C). The control E. coli cells were full-bodied, with a typical long rod shape, and the slight surface wrinkling probably originated from the flagellar structure; the treated E. coli cells showed no apparent deformation or wrinkling. The TEM images visually demonstrated the internal structure of S. aureus. Compared with the regular control group (Fig. 2c), the cell wall of IMZ-GQDs-treated S. aureus (Fig. 2d) was highly disrupted, showing a disordered internal structure with cytoplasmic exudation around the cell.

The cell wall plays an essential role in bacterial growth and metabolism, and any physical or chemical damage to it may eventually lead to bacterial dysfunction and death. The small size of the cationic nanomaterials has been shown to contribute to the diffusion of the material itself and the subsequent electrostatic interaction with negatively charged bacterial surfaces, thus exhibiting excellent bacterial adhesion and penetration and finally causing cell wall irregularities and breakage (Gao et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019).

In combination with the results obtained in the previous section, it could be inferred that the IMZ-GQDs showed relatively strong antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria and that there is a commonality among the different strains, i.e., causing bacterial death by disrupting the cell wall integrity.

To demonstrate the antibacterial activity of IMZ-GQDs related to the positively charged properties, negatively charged aminated GQDs were set as a comparison. From Fig. 3a and b, the inhibitory effect of negatively charged aminated GQDs (zeta potential ca. − 3.7 mV) was much lower than that of positively charged IMZ-GQDs (zeta potential ca. + 32.2 mV), and the corresponding survival rates of S. aureus were 92.1% and 1.4%, respectively. The results signify that the surface charge of GQDs plays an important role in the antibacterial mechanism, which is consistent with the findings of previous literature (Li et al., 2016).

IMZ-GQDs derive their positive charge from the imidazole group contained therein. The specific amphiphilic structure of imidazolium salts can be achieved with alkylation of the imidazole ring with a hydrophilic cation head and hydrophobic substitutions (Riduan and Zhang, 2013). The abundance of phosphate groups in the cell wall gives bacteria a negative surface charge, thus allowing the hydrophilic cationic segments of imidazole rings to adhere to the bacterial surface via electrostatic interaction, coupled with disturbances of the structure and function by the hydrophobic segments (Cornellas et al., 2011; Garcia et al., 2013).

The bacterial membrane potential is maintained from −140 to −75 mV, which is crucial for multiple aspects of physiological function, such as the driving force for energy translation, localization of proteins, mediation of intracellular and intercellular signaling, etc. (Felle et al., 1980; Strahl and Hamoen, 2010). Importantly, cationic nanomaterials have been shown to disrupt the cell wall integrity while causing an uncontrolled influx/efflux of ions across the plasma membrane and cessation of the proton gradient across it (Wang et al., 2017).

To further evaluate the effect of IMZ-GQDs on the bacterial inner membrane, the carbocyanine dye DiOC2(3) was used to detect and analyze the membrane potential change in the bacteria. DiOC2(3) exhibits green fluorescence in all bacterial cells, whereas the dye molecules self-associate under larger membrane potentials in healthy membrane so that a red shift occurs. Therefore, the red/green fluorescence ratio allows measurement of the membrane potential in the bacteria.

As seen in Fig. 3c and d, the red and green fluorescence was equally distributed in the control group, with a red/green ratio of 1.04, thereby indicating that the bacteria maintained a high level of membrane potential. CCCP, a proton ionophore, was set as a positive control that destroys the membrane potential by eliminating the proton gradient (Novo et al., 2000).The CCCP-treated S. aureus displayed almost no red fluorescence, and the red/green ratio decreased to 0.03 as a result of depolarized bacterial membranes. The red fluorescence of IMZ-GQDs-treated S. aureus was also significantly reduced compared with that of the control, with a red/green ratio of 0.20, thus signifying a decline in membrane potential. Specifically, the loss of membrane integrity led to the changes in membrane permeability and uncontrolled ion transport, thereby inducing membrane potential dissipation (Gao et al., 2020).

The abovementioned results indicate that the positive surface charge of IMZ-GQDs is an important factor in the antibacterial process. Additionally, nitrogen- and oxygen-doped carbon nanomaterials possess enzyme-like activity for generating intracellular ROS in the presence of H2O2 or O2 molecules, with or without light irradiation (Liu et al., 2011; Meziani et al., 2016). Altogether, without extra stimulation, the nanosized IMZ-GQDs adhere to and penetrate the negatively charged bacterial surfaces via electrostatic interaction. They disrupt the cell wall integrity mainly because of the cationic properties of the imidazole ring, thus resulting in the leakage of cellular components, decline in membrane potential, and, eventually, bacterial death.

To prepare the IMZ-GQDs MNs with IMZ-GQDs concentrated at the tip of the MN shaft, a typical two-step micromolding method was employed (Fig. 4a) by first casting the IMZ-GQDs solution into the PDMS mold, followed by a mixture of PVA/PVP solution. The needles were well arranged into a 5 × 5 MN array of uniform sizes and morphologies in the microscopic images (Fig. 4b and c, Fig. S5 a and b); compared with the colorless transparency of Blank MNs, IMZ-GQDs MNs had a slight brown color owing to the loading of IMZ-GQDs. As shown in the SEM images (Fig. 4d and S5c), each cone had smooth edges and a sharp tip, with similar height and base width (Table S1) in IMZ-GQDs MNs (319.00 and 190.00 µm) and Blank MNs (298.00 and 187.67 µm). Owing to the evaporation of water during the fabrication, the obtained MNs were smaller than the PDMS mold.
IMZ-GQDs MNs are expected to dissolve in the corneal stroma after penetrating the tear film and corneal epithelial layer because of their appropriate dissolving and penetrating properties.

First, 20% ethanol/water (v/v) solution was chosen to simulate the cornea (ca. 81.6% water content) for in vitro dissolving experiments (Krause, 1932). PVA and PVP are both polymers commonly used in the preparation of dissolving MNs; thus, IMZ-GQDs MNs and Blank MNs became slightly softened within 30 s after exposure to the medium and were generally dissolved within 2 min (Fig. 5A and S6a). In Fig. S7, after 30 s of application, the dissolving MN hydrated quickly and adhered to the ocular surface due to the absorption of tear fluid, and the substrate part starts to thicken after 2 min. These results reflect the good solubility of the obtained MNs.

The Parafilm® M (125 µm in thickness) was much stronger than the cornea in mechanical aspects and was commonly used to simulate transdermal experiments; thus, we stacked multiple layers of Parafilm® M to assess the in vitro penetration of MNs (Caffarel-Salvador et al., 2015; Matthews et al., 2014). As shown in Fig. 5B and S6b, both IMZ-GQDs MNs and Blank MNs left a clear 5 × 5 array of cavities at 125 and 250 µm, with a decreasing hole area because of the cone morphology. For the 375-µm depth, only dot-like traces were left on the Parafilm® M with an incomplete array as the MNs were not able to penetrate this thickness because of their height (ca. 300 µm). To enter the corneal stroma, the insert depth of MNs should be between the thickness of the corneal epithelium and the full corneal layer (50–550 µm); thus, the insert depth of IMZ-GQDs MNs was sufficient.

To further confirm the insertion capability of IMZ-GQDs MNs, representative slit-lamp biomicroscopic images were acquired before and after insertion (Fig. 5C). After the application of IMZ-GQDs MNs for 2 min, the punctured cornea showed 5 × 5 well-defined pinholes with visible fluorescein sodium staining under cobalt blue light. This finding demonstrated that IMZ-GQDs MNs had caused defects in the corneal epithelium after 2 min of compression. After 24 h, the application site recovered to its intact original state without insertion or staining marks, which signified that the epithelial defects caused by IMZ-GQDs MNs are self-healing and do not cause persistent damage to the rabbit cornea. The recovery of ocular barrier was consistent with previous reports. It could be explained by the high regenerative capacity of the corneal epithelium, which could restore quickly by cell division (Kumar et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2022; Than et al., 2018). Summarizing the above, the low invasiveness of IMZ-GQDs MNs is both valid and safe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity of IMZ-GQDs MNs
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the effect of the MNs fabrication process on the in vitro antibacterial activity of IMZ-GQDs, the obtained MNs were dissolved for characterization (Fig. S8). The survival rate of S. aureus in the Blank MNs group (95.4%) was not statistically different from that of the Blank control group (92.5%), which indicated that the PVP/PVA mixture did not inherently exert an antibacterial effect. Only 2.5% of S. aureus cells survived in the IMZ-GQDs MNs group, which was similar to the rate of the IMZ-GQDs control group (1.9%), thereby excluding the impact of vacuum drying and PVP/PVA blends on the original antibacterial properties of IMZ-GQDs. IMZ-GQDs MNs maintained a powerful antibacterial activity that was not inferior to that of IMZ-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and in vivo ocular biocompatibility of IMZ-GQDs MNs
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm whether IMZ-GQDs, Blank MNs, and IMZ-GQDs MNs were cytotoxic to physiological tissues, we prepared IMZ-GQDs solutions of different concentrations and dissolved MNs in an aqueous solution to evaluate their cytotoxicity toward human corneal epithelial cells (HCECs) with cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) and live/dead staining. The solution and its dilutions were incubated with the cells for 24 h. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the survival rate of HCECs was 89.2% after coculture at a concentration of 100 µg/mL, and the rates at other concentrations were also > 80%. In the case of HCECs cocultured with Blank MNs and IMZ-GQDs MNs, the cell viability was 96.9% and 88.6%, respectively, with no significant effect on the HCECs growth.

The live/dead viability assay with Calcein-AM/PI double staining further revealed that IMZ-GQDs, Blank MNs, and IMZ-GQDs MNs did not cause considerable cytotoxicity to HCECs. Calcein-AM is a nonfluorescent dye that easily permeates live mammalian cells with an intact cell membrane and emits a strong green fluorescence. PI could only enter cells with damaged cell membranes and bind to nucleic acids, thereby producing a strong red fluorescence. As illustrated in Fig. 6B, the pronounced green fluorescence and sparse red fluorescence of the stained cells showed no significant changes in both morphology and the state of the HCECs. The abovementioned results indicate that IMZ-GQDs and MNs solutions are not cytotoxic to HCECs and could be safely applied to the ocular surface.

After the in vitro cytotoxicity test of IMZ-GQDs, we subsequently evaluated another important property, their biocompatibility for in vivo applications. Herein, we examined the rabbit eye responses to ocular surface pressing with IMZ-GQDs MNs. The morphological and structural changes in the corneal and retinal tissues were observed using a slit-lamp biomicroscope and a retinal microscope, respectively. During the follow-up period of 14 days, the cornea of both the control and treated groups was smooth and clear, without fluorescein sodium staining or severe anterior segment tissue responses. These findings indicate the absence of sustained defects on the corneal epithelium because of the low invasiveness of IMZ-GQDs MNs (Fig. 6C a). Meanwhile, the retinal microscope results showed that the treated rabbit retina remained flat and the optic papillae were well-defined, without hemorrhage, exudation, or pathological changes in vessels. Thus, the micro application of pressure on the ocular surface did not produce visible effects on the retinal tissue (Fig. 6C b). Our results clearly demonstrate that the low invasiveness and slight pressure of IMZ-GQDs MNs are highly biocompatible and do not initiate a tissue response.

In the intraocular treatment study, the S. aureus-induced BK model was established with intrastromal injection to examine the therapeutic efficacy of IMZ-GQDs MNs in rabbit eyes. The animals were grouped 6 h after molding (as described in the Methods). Of these, the ofloxacin0.3% and ofloxacin0.01% groups were administered with commercially available ofloxacin eye drops (3 mg/mL) and diluted ofloxacin eye drops (100 µg/mL), respectively. The alterations in anterior segment morphometry in each group were studied at a fixed time on days 1, 3, 5, 7, 10, and 14 (Fig. 7 ).

As observed with slit-lamp biomicroscopy, on the 1st day, all groups presented with severe inflammatory responses, including congestion and edema of the conjunctiva coupled with staining of corneal defects at the inoculation site. The therapeutic efficacy of IMZ-GQDs MNs and ofloxacin0.3% became prominent on day 3 after the onset of symptoms. Obvious relief from inflammatory reactions and markedly reduced area of fluorescein sodium staining were noted. In contrast, the remaining groups showed varying degrees of corneal infiltration, clouding, and anterior chamber hypopyon, implying that IMZ-GQDs and ofloxacin0.01% eye drop treatments produced little therapeutic effects.

By day 7, the anterior segments of the IMZ-GQDs MNs and ofloxacin0.3% groups had almost returned to normal (clear corneas and quiet anterior chambers), with only slight infiltration at the injection sites. At 7–14 days, when the anterior chamber hypopyon was gradually absorbed, the corneal edema subsided and transparency was slowly restored in the resting groups. However, more severe symptoms appeared because of continued inflammation; the chronic hypoxic state of the cornea resulted in corneal ulcer and neovascularization. The symptoms were relatively mild in the IMZ-GQDs eye drop group, probably because of the superior antimicrobial effect of IMZ-GQDs, but they were most severe in the control group in whom the corneal ulcer eventually progressed to perforations.

The overall ocular scores (Fig. 8 a) in the IMZ-GQDs MNs and ofloxacin0.3% groups were significantly lower than those in the control and Blank MNs groups during the entire study period. Ofloxacin is a widely used broad-spectrum antibiotic that has been extensively verified to be efficient in the clinical treatment of BK. However, the clinical application of ofloxacin eye drops often requires high doses and high frequency of spotting, which increase the risk of the development of side effects and antibiotic resistance (Goldstein et al., 1999). From the scores, although IMZ-GQDs MNs was slightly less effective than ofloxacin0.3% in the early phase of treatment (before day 7), its efficiency was similar to that of ofloxacin0.3% as a whole and can be applied at a much lower concentration (100 µg/mL) than ofloxacin0.3% (3 mg/mL). For comparison, the treatment efficiency of ofloxacin0.01% at the same concentration (100 µg/mL) was further tested; the IMZ-GQDs MNs showed significantly superior activity compared with ofloxacin0.01% since day 3. Additionally, the therapeutic efficacy of IMZ-GQDs eye drops was also better than that of ofloxacin0.01%, which further demonstrated the superiority of IMZ-GQDs antimicrobial activity, but it was not as effective as the same dose of IMZ-GQDs MNs. The reason for this disparity may be the different administration modes. Specifically, the eye drops can stay on the ocular surface only momentarily because of tear metabolism and anatomical barrier, whereas MNs mechanically pierce the ocular surface and directly act on the stroma, thereby resulting in better therapeutic effects.

As shown in Fig. S9, the ocular surface secretion was cultured on blood agar plates overnight. Almost no colony formation was observed in the IMZ-GQDs MNs and ofloxacin0.3% groups as compared with the control and Blank MNs groups, which further supported their high antibacterial activity. Some colonies were produced in the ofloxacin0.01% group before day 7 owing to the poor antibacterial effect. Interestingly, the mismatch between a few colonies and relatively severe symptoms in the IMZ-GQDs eye drop group was due to the fact that the eye drops were not able to kill the intrastromal bacteria. Corneal pathological observation of each group was performed on day 14. As shown in Fig. 8b, destruction of the stromal tissue matrix and severe inflammatory cell infiltration were observed in the cornea of the control, Blank MNs, IMZ-GQDs eye drop, and ofloxacin0.01% groups, with varying degrees of corneal thickening and neovascularization. Contrastingly, there were very few infiltrating inflammatory cells in the IMZ-GQDs MNs and ofloxacin0.3% groups, and the corneal architecture was maintained without significant thickening or defect.

In brief, based on its superior antibacterial and penetrating properties, the IMZ-GQDs MNs, with smaller dosage and lower risk of drug resistance, is comparable to commercial ofloxacin0.3% for the treatment of S. aureus-induced BK in rabbit eyes. Our study was currently limited to the ocular surface, and the application of microneedles on other delivery barriers of eye will be performed in subsequent experiments. Taking together, this idea of combining nanomaterials with microneedle technology could draw on their strengths and thus provide a new therapeutic strategy for various superficial diseases.

We developed an MN patch loaded with IMZ-GQDs in PVA/PVP matrix with improved antibacterial activity and bioavailability using a micromolding method. As the antibacterial component of IMZ-GQDs MNs, cationic IMZ-GQDs exhibited outstanding antibacterial activity against three Gram-positive strains (S. aureus, S. epidermidis, and MRSA) owing to the loss of membrane integrity and decline in membrane potential. Additionally, because of their MN form, IMZ-GQDs MNs also benefits from the microneedle form, showed adequate dissolution and penetration to overcome the rabbit corneal epithelial barrier and get inserted into the stroma. The excellent biocompatibility of IMZ-GQDs MNs was verified both in vitro and in vivo. Furthermore, the topical application of IMZ-GQDs MNs led to the resolution of S. aureus-induced BK in rabbits within 7 days. The efficiency was comparable to that of commercial 0.3% ofloxacin but was much superior to the eye drop mode itself. Predictably, the combination of nanotechnology and MNs would overturn the therapeutic modes of various ophthalmic diseases, not just BK, in the future.

Yirong Fang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lin Zhuo: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Hang Yuan: Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation. Hao Zhao: Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation. Lishu Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
